title: Menorrhagia
oneLiner: Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) – affects 1 in 3 women – common cause of iron‐deficiency anaemia and impaired quality of life.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Haemorrhagic shock (acute severe bleed):** Any woman with tachycardia ± hypotension, pallor, and soaking through a pad every 15–20 minutes → check pulse, BP, active resuscitation needed before any history.
- **Underlying bleeding disorder (e.g. von Willebrand disease, platelet dysfunction):** Look for easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, family history, or use of anticoagulants → immediate coagulation screen.
- **Pregnancy complication (ectopic or miscarriage):** Always do a pregnancy test in reproductive age – adnexal tenderness/syncope + positive test → urgent gynaecology referral.
sections.Presentation:
- Heavy, prolonged (>7 days) or frequent (<21 days) menstrual bleeding
- Flooding (pad changes every 1–2 hours) or large clots (>2.5 cm)
- Intermenstrual bleeding (if present, raises suspicion for structural pathology)
- Fatigue, dizziness, breathlessness (anaemia)
- Pelvic pain/pressure (fibroids, adenomyosis)

Key exam findings:
- Conjunctival pallor, tachycardia (if acute anaemia)
- Speculum exam: cervical lesions, visible polyps, bleeding from os
- Bimanual: enlarged uterus (fibroids, adenomyosis), adnexal masses
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Uterine fibroids** – most common structural cause; enlarged irregular uterus, heavy periods often with pain
- **Adenomyosis** – tender, diffusely enlarged uterus; heavy + dysmenorrhoea
- **Endometrial polyps** – intermenstrual bleeding; normal exam but ultrasound shows echogenic mass
- **Endometrial hyperplasia/cancer** – postmenopausal bleeding or age >45 with risk factors (obesity, PCOS, nulliparity)
- **Coagulopathy** – menarche onset + bruising/family history; lab evidence
- **Thyroid dysfunction** – hypothyroidism associated with menorrhagia; TSH routinely checked
- **Anovulatory cycles** (perimenarche/perimenopause) – irregular, unpredictable heavy bleeding
- **Cervical pathology** – contact bleeding, abnormal smear history

**Splitter:** Intermenstrual bleeding + negative ultrasound → suspect endometrial or cervical pathology (hysteroscopy required).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine/serum β‐HCG (all reproductive‐age women)
  - Vital signs, speculum ± bimanual exam
  - Hb check (point‐of‐care if available)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, MCV, platelets)
  - Ferritin (even if Hb normal – iron deficiency common)
  - Coagulation screen (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen) if bruising or suspicion of coagulopathy
  - TSH (if cycle irregular or clinical signs of thyroid disease)
  - Consider von Willebrand screen (vW factor, ristocetin cofactor) if menarche onset
- **Imaging:**
  - First‑line: transvaginal pelvic ultrasound (TVUS) – assesses endometrial thickness, fibroids, polyps, adenomyosis
  - Second‑line: hysteroscopy ± biopsy if TVUS abnormal or endometrial thickness >4 mm in postmenopausal women (or >12 mm in premenopausal with risk factors)
- **Special tests:**
  - Endometrial biopsy if age >45, intermenstrual bleeding, or thickened endometrium
  - MRI pelvis if fibroid mapping needed or suspected adenomyosis
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE quality standard: HMB defined by impact on quality of life, not objective blood loss
  - Pictorial Blood Loss Assessment Chart (PBAC) score >100 = objective heavy bleeding (but rarely used in acute setting)
- **Never‑miss test:** Pregnancy test (urine/serum) in all women of reproductive age – omitting it can miss ectopic or early miscarriage causing haemorrhage.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable: IV access × 2, crystalloid bolus (500 mL stat), cross‑match, call gynaecology reg
  - If stable but acute severe bleed: **tranexamic acid 1 g IV or PO** (first line to reduce bleeding)
  - Consider **mefenamic acid 500 mg PO** (antiprostaglandin effect)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - If admitted for anaemia: oral ferrous sulphate 200 mg TDS (or IV iron if severe intolerance/rapid correction needed)
  - If ongoing heavy bleeding despite tranexamic acid: **norethisterone 10 mg TDS** (cyclically) – [check local policy]
  - Arrange TVUS ± outpatient hysteroscopy
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - First‑line medical: **Levonorgestrel‑releasing intrauterine system (Mirena®)** – reduces bleeding by 80–90%
  - Alternatives: combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP), cyclical progestogens (norethisterone 5 mg TDS days 5–26), tranexamic acid (1 g TDS during menses)
  - Surgical: endometrial ablation (if childbearing complete), myomectomy/hysterectomy for fibroids
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Baseline vital signs 4‑hourly, record pad count
  - IV fluids: crystalloid maintenance (1–2 L/24 h) if NBM or dehydrated
  - VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated by active bleeding
  - Diet: light, encourage iron‑rich foods
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Tranexamic acid** – 1 g orally three times daily during menstruation (max 4 g/day) OR 1 g IV over 10 minutes for acute bleed
- **Mefenamic acid** – 500 mg orally three times daily with food (during menses); avoid if peptic ulcer/hypersensitivity to NSAIDs
- **Norethisterone** – 5 mg orally three times daily from days 5–26 of cycle for HMB; for acute heavy bleeding may use 10 mg TDS for 10 days then reduce – [check local policy]
- **Levonorgestrel‑IUS (Mirena®)** – 20 μg/day released; inserted by trained practitioner, lasts 5 years
- **Ferrous sulphate** – 200 mg orally TDS (check Hb after 4 weeks)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Tranexamic acid: caution in thromboembolic disease, recent MI
  - Mefenamic acid: avoid with severe asthma, renal impairment, pregnant/breastfeeding
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No major adjustment for most above; NSAIDs require dose reduction in renal impairment; tranexamic acid reduce dose in severe renal failure (CrCl <30: consider not using)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting **norethisterone 10 mg TDS indefinitely** without checking BMI/liver function – risk of thrombosis; always prescribe on a cyclical schedule (days 5–26) unless urgent scenario.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhagic shock (rare if transient)
  - Severe anaemia (Hb <70 g/L) – transfusion may be required
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (most common)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic fatigue, impaired work/social life
  - Infertility (if underlying cause like endometriosis/fibroids)
  - Endometrial hyperplasia/cancer risk (especially if anovulatory cycles)
sections.Prognosis:
- Most women respond well – 80% reduction in bleeding with Mirena IUS within 6 months
- If untreated, iron deficiency anaemia progressive; small fraction develop endometrial cancer (age >45 risk)
- No increased mortality if underlying causes are ruled out
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always assess endometrial thickness on TVUS** – even in young women, a thin stripe (<5 mm) largely rules out cancer/hyperplasia.
- **Check ferritin** in every woman with menorrhagia and normal Hb – iron deficiency is very common and treatable.
- **Offer Mirena IUS as first line** to women who have completed childbearing – it is more effective than oral therapies.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Skipping pregnancy test** in any reproductive‑age woman – miss ectopic or miscarriage → disaster.
- **Relying on Hb alone** – a normal Hb does not rule out iron deficiency; check ferritin.
- **Prescribing tranexamic acid for >5 days** without reviewing bleeding pattern – risk of thrombotic events (rare but important).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Soaking through a pad every hour for >2 hours
  - Dizziness, feeling faint, or shortness of breath
  - Severe pelvic pain or fever (suspect infection if structural cause)
- **Follow‑up:**
  - GP in 2 weeks to review Hb, ferritin and initiate long‑term treatment
  - Gynaecology clinic within 6 weeks if TVUS abnormal or no response to first‑line medical therapy
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call gynaecology registrar if:
  - Haemodynamically unstable (systolic BP <90, HR >110)
  - Hb <70 g/L with ongoing bleeding
  - Suspected ectopic pregnancy (positive test + adnexal tenderness + free fluid on ultrasound)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
- ICU referral if massive transfusion required or ongoing haemorrhagic shock despite initial resuscitation.

rawMarkdown:
## Menorrhagia

**One-liner:** Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) – affects 1 in 3 women – common cause of iron‐deficiency anaemia and impaired quality of life.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Haemorrhagic shock (acute severe bleed):** Any woman with tachycardia ± hypotension, pallor, and soaking through a pad every 15–20 minutes → check pulse, BP, active resuscitation needed before any history.
- **Underlying bleeding disorder (e.g. von Willebrand disease, platelet dysfunction):** Look for easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, family history, or use of anticoagulants → immediate coagulation screen.
- **Pregnancy complication (ectopic or miscarriage):** Always do a pregnancy test in reproductive age – adnexal tenderness/syncope + positive test → urgent gynaecology referral.

### Presentation
- Heavy, prolonged (>7 days) or frequent (<21 days) menstrual bleeding
- Flooding (pad changes every 1–2 hours) or large clots (>2.5 cm)
- Intermenstrual bleeding (if present, raises suspicion for structural pathology)
- Fatigue, dizziness, breathlessness (anaemia)
- Pelvic pain/pressure (fibroids, adenomyosis)

Key exam findings:
- Conjunctival pallor, tachycardia (if acute anaemia)
- Speculum exam: cervical lesions, visible polyps, bleeding from os
- Bimanual: enlarged uterus (fibroids, adenomyosis), adnexal masses

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Uterine fibroids** – most common structural cause; enlarged irregular uterus, heavy periods often with pain
- **Adenomyosis** – tender, diffusely enlarged uterus; heavy + dysmenorrhoea
- **Endometrial polyps** – intermenstrual bleeding; normal exam but ultrasound shows echogenic mass
- **Endometrial hyperplasia/cancer** – postmenopausal bleeding or age >45 with risk factors (obesity, PCOS, nulliparity)
- **Coagulopathy** – menarche onset + bruising/family history; lab evidence
- **Thyroid dysfunction** – hypothyroidism associated with menorrhagia; TSH routinely checked
- **Anovulatory cycles** (perimenarche/perimenopause) – irregular, unpredictable heavy bleeding
- **Cervical pathology** – contact bleeding, abnormal smear history

**Splitter:** Intermenstrual bleeding + negative ultrasound → suspect endometrial or cervical pathology (hysteroscopy required).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine/serum β‐HCG (all reproductive‐age women)
  - Vital signs, speculum ± bimanual exam
  - Hb check (point‐of‐care if available)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, MCV, platelets)
  - Ferritin (even if Hb normal – iron deficiency common)
  - Coagulation screen (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen) if bruising or suspicion of coagulopathy
  - TSH (if cycle irregular or clinical signs of thyroid disease)
  - Consider von Willebrand screen (vW factor, ristocetin cofactor) if menarche onset
- **Imaging:**
  - First‑line: transvaginal pelvic ultrasound (TVUS) – assesses endometrial thickness, fibroids, polyps, adenomyosis
  - Second‑line: hysteroscopy ± biopsy if TVUS abnormal or endometrial thickness >4 mm in postmenopausal women (or >12 mm in premenopausal with risk factors)
- **Special tests:**
  - Endometrial biopsy if age >45, intermenstrual bleeding, or thickened endometrium
  - MRI pelvis if fibroid mapping needed or suspected adenomyosis
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE quality standard: HMB defined by impact on quality of life, not objective blood loss
  - Pictorial Blood Loss Assessment Chart (PBAC) score >100 = objective heavy bleeding (but rarely used in acute setting)
- **Never‑miss test:** Pregnancy test (urine/serum) in all women of reproductive age – omitting it can miss ectopic or early miscarriage causing haemorrhage.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable: IV access × 2, crystalloid bolus (500 mL stat), cross‑match, call gynaecology reg
  - If stable but acute severe bleed: **tranexamic acid 1 g IV or PO** (first line to reduce bleeding)
  - Consider **mefenamic acid 500 mg PO** (antiprostaglandin effect)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - If admitted for anaemia: oral ferrous sulphate 200 mg TDS (or IV iron if severe intolerance/rapid correction needed)
  - If ongoing heavy bleeding despite tranexamic acid: **norethisterone 10 mg TDS** (cyclically) – [check local policy]
  - Arrange TVUS ± outpatient hysteroscopy
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - First‑line medical: **Levonorgestrel‑releasing intrauterine system (Mirena®)** – reduces bleeding by 80–90%
  - Alternatives: combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP), cyclical progestogens (norethisterone 5 mg TDS days 5–26), tranexamic acid (1 g TDS during menses)
  - Surgical: endometrial ablation (if childbearing complete), myomectomy/hysterectomy for fibroids
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Baseline vital signs 4‑hourly, record pad count
  - IV fluids: crystalloid maintenance (1–2 L/24 h) if NBM or dehydrated
  - VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated by active bleeding
  - Diet: light, encourage iron‑rich foods

### Drugs & Doses
- **Tranexamic acid** – 1 g orally three times daily during menstruation (max 4 g/day) OR 1 g IV over 10 minutes for acute bleed
- **Mefenamic acid** – 500 mg orally three times daily with food (during menses); avoid if peptic ulcer/hypersensitivity to NSAIDs
- **Norethisterone** – 5 mg orally three times daily from days 5–26 of cycle for HMB; for acute heavy bleeding may use 10 mg TDS for 10 days then reduce – [check local policy]
- **Levonorgestrel‑IUS (Mirena®)** – 20 μg/day released; inserted by trained practitioner, lasts 5 years
- **Ferrous sulphate** – 200 mg orally TDS (check Hb after 4 weeks)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Tranexamic acid: caution in thromboembolic disease, recent MI
  - Mefenamic acid: avoid with severe asthma, renal impairment, pregnant/breastfeeding
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No major adjustment for most above; NSAIDs require dose reduction in renal impairment; tranexamic acid reduce dose in severe renal failure (CrCl <30: consider not using)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting **norethisterone 10 mg TDS indefinitely** without checking BMI/liver function – risk of thrombosis; always prescribe on a cyclical schedule (days 5–26) unless urgent scenario.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhagic shock (rare if transient)
  - Severe anaemia (Hb <70 g/L) – transfusion may be required
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (most common)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic fatigue, impaired work/social life
  - Infertility (if underlying cause like endometriosis/fibroids)
  - Endometrial hyperplasia/cancer risk (especially if anovulatory cycles)

### Prognosis
- Most women respond well – 80% reduction in bleeding with Mirena IUS within 6 months
- If untreated, iron deficiency anaemia progressive; small fraction develop endometrial cancer (age >45 risk)
- No increased mortality if underlying causes are ruled out

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always assess endometrial thickness on TVUS** – even in young women, a thin stripe (<5 mm) largely rules out cancer/hyperplasia.
- **Check ferritin** in every woman with menorrhagia and normal Hb – iron deficiency is very common and treatable.
- **Offer Mirena IUS as first line** to women who have completed childbearing – it is more effective than oral therapies.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Skipping pregnancy test** in any reproductive‑age woman – miss ectopic or miscarriage → disaster.
- **Relying on Hb alone** – a normal Hb does not rule out iron deficiency; check ferritin.
- **Prescribing tranexamic acid for >5 days** without reviewing bleeding pattern – risk of thrombotic events (rare but important).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Soaking through a pad every hour for >2 hours
  - Dizziness, feeling faint, or shortness of breath
  - Severe pelvic pain or fever (suspect infection if structural cause)
- **Follow‑up:**
  - GP in 2 weeks to review Hb, ferritin and initiate long‑term treatment
  - Gynaecology clinic within 6 weeks if TVUS abnormal or no response to first‑line medical therapy

### Escalation Criteria
- Call gynaecology registrar if:
  - Haemodynamically unstable (systolic BP <90, HR >110)
  - Hb <70 g/L with ongoing bleeding
  - Suspected ectopic pregnancy (positive test + adnexal tenderness + free fluid on ultrasound)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
- ICU referral if massive transfusion required or ongoing haemorrhagic shock despite initial resuscitation.
